lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Anatomy of the particle
Alright, with that context, let me show you what a retrovirus actually looks like.
A retroviral particle — a virion — is roughly spherical, about 100 nanometers across.
So you'd fit a thousand of them across the width of a human hair.
They are enveloped, meaning the outer layer of the particle is a lipid bilayer membrane, which the virus steals from the host cell on its way out.
Studded in that membrane are the viral envelope glycoproteins.
We'll come back to those when we talk about entry.
Inside the membrane is a layer called the matrix, made of the matrix protein MA.
The matrix sits between the membrane and the next structure inward, which is the capsid — CA protein — and the capsid is the iconic structure.
In HIV-1, the mature capsid is a conical structure that looks, in cryo-electron tomograms, like a tapered ice cream cone.
In murine leukemia virus and most other retroviruses it's roughly spherical.
Either way, the capsid encloses the genome and the viral enzymes.
Now, inside the capsid you have the genome itself.
Here's something a little unusual: the genome of a retrovirus is two copies of single-stranded, positive-sense RNA, each about 7 to 10 kilobases long, joined near their 5' ends by base-pairing.
So a retrovirus is diploid — it carries two genome copies per virion.
This is going to matter when we talk about recombination during reverse transcription.
The two RNAs are coated by the nucleocapsid protein, NC, which is a small basic protein with zinc-finger motifs that grips the RNA.
Packaged into the virion alongside the genome are three viral enzymes that the particle absolutely must bring with it, because the host cell does not provide them.
These are:
Reverse transcriptase, RT — the enzyme of the family name.
It is an RNA- and DNA-dependent DNA polymerase with an intrinsic ribonuclease H activity.
It walks down an RNA template laying down DNA, and along the way it can chew up the RNA template it has just used.
We will spend an entire segment on what this enzyme does because it is, frankly, beautiful and a little bit insane.

Integrase, IN — the enzyme that takes the double-stranded DNA product of reverse transcription, finds an accessible spot in a host chromosome, and covalently inserts the viral DNA into the host genome.
Protease, PR — a small aspartyl protease that cleaves the viral polyproteins as the new particle matures.
Without protease, you get immature, non-infectious particles.
We'll come back to this when we discuss protease inhibitors.
So that's the cargo.
Lipid envelope studded with Env glycoproteins on the outside.
Matrix under the membrane.
Capsid in the middle.
Diploid RNA genome and three enzymes inside.
That is a complete retroviral particle.
Now, two structural notes that matter clinically and conceptually.
First — the diploid genome.
Why does the virus carry two copies?
It looks wasteful.
It turns out that during reverse transcription, the enzyme can jump from one RNA template to the other.

If those two RNAs carry slightly different sequences — for instance, if a cell was co-infected by two different HIV variants and packaged one copy of each into the same virion — then RT can mix and match, producing a recombinant DNA product.
This is retroviral recombination, and it is one of the reasons HIV evolves drug resistance so fast.
Two genome copies per particle is the structural basis for one of the most clinically relevant features of the family.
Second — those Env glycoproteins on the surface.
There are two of them, SU (surface) and TM (transmembrane), and they are cleaved from a single Env precursor by a host enzyme called furin in the late secretory pathway.
SU sits on the outside and recognizes the host receptor.
TM anchors the complex in the membrane and contains the fusion peptide, which buries itself in the host membrane during entry to drive fusion.
In HIV-1, SU is gp120 and TM is gp41 — the numbers refer to the molecular weights in kilodaltons.
Together they form a trimer of heterodimers on the surface, a tripod-like spike with three gp120s on top and three gp41s as the legs.
That's the particle.
Now let's open the genome and look at what it encodes.
